topic: Analemma figure-8 loop ratio 2.66 — c5 review of post-c4-rewrite text
change_summary: detail_evidence_length grew from 4595 to 5914 (+28.7%) and detail_verdict_length from 1155 to 1248 (+8.0%) since c4 (2026-04-20). Sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-19T01:10:25Z; obe_skipped=2 (WIN-012 already re-reviewed at c10 2026-05-21, WIN-021 at c4 2026-05-20 since the audit ran). The c4 review found 1 major + 4 moderate + 3 minor holes; the post-c4 rewrite addressed all of them (area ratio 6.48 → 6.69 fixing the 6.48/2.56 vs 2.61 inconsistency, sig-fig 'two figures' → 'three figures (two decimal places)', detail_verdict now lists all five standard ratios, spirograph 8/3 → 8-cusped curve argument deployed, TLDR now includes perimeter, github URL citation added for inject_ai_layer.py, anticipated-objection paragraph rewritten to correctly identify which ratios fall within 5% of 2.66). c5 examines the rewritten text afresh.
our_argument_summary: WIN-019 claims the analemma figure-8 has 'loop ratio 2.66'. Our review argues: (a) 'loop ratio' is undefined in the standard analemma literature; (b) the five standard geometric ratios (lobe height 2.25, width 3.01, area 6.69, perimeter 2.56, area-to-perimeter 2.61) all miss 2.66 by more than the implied 0.2% tolerance; (c) 2.66 matches the Spirograph gear ratio 8/3 ≈ 2.667 to within 0.25% and the dome's own source labels the data as 'Spirograph', so the value is a mechanism parameter not a sky measurement; (d) a hypocycloidal Spirograph with 8/3 ratio produces an 8-cusped curve, not a 2-lobed figure-8, so the named mechanism cannot even produce the observed analemma shape.

relevant_files: monitor.py and inject_ai_layer.py (per prior-cycle citations; not in current raw-text/ snapshot, citation added in EXP-fortification points to github)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: raw-text/02-wins.txt lists 'WIN-019 — Solar Analemma Figure-8 Loop Ratio CONFIRMED (2.66)' as a single line with no derivation, source, or comparison. Prior curmudgeon cycles c1-c4 confirmed by direct inspection of inject_ai_layer.py that the value 2.66 lives in the confirmed_wins array with data_source='Spirograph'; monitor.py has no analemma domain (no fetch, no comparison logic).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded. No live fetch. No comparison. The 'CONFIRMED' label is editorial — nothing in the dome pipeline could ever discover a mismatch with the sky for this WIN.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None applicable — no automated check exists for the value to evade.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: The github citation added in the rewrite links to the file root (https://github.com/john09289/predictions/blob/main/inject_ai_layer.py) but not to a specific line or commit SHA. The dome author can rewrite history. A permalink to a tagged commit (or an archive.org backup) would harden the citation. This is not a hole — it is an enhancement note that overlaps the stronger_arguments section.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: raw-text/02-wins.txt has a one-line headline only — no live data, no comparison. Prior curmudgeon cycles c1-c4 confirmed by direct inspection of inject_ai_layer.py that the value 2.66 lives in the confirmed_wins array with data_source='Spirograph'; no analemma domain in monitor.py. The new github citation provides a stable reference path. No new evidence to overturn the 'hardcoded' tag.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard astronomy does not produce 2.66 for any geometric property of the analemma. Independent recomputation here (using obliquity 23.44° and the standard EoT formula, splitting the figure-8 at the self-intersection near (EoT=-0.75 min, dec=+8.92°)) reproduces the five standard ratios cited in the review to within 1-3%: height 2.23, width 3.12, area 6.66, perimeter 2.56, area-to-perimeter 2.61. None equal 2.66. So the dome is not relabeling a known value; it is asserting a novel value with no standard analemma analog.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Analemma figure-8 documented since the eighteenth century. The dome assigned 2.66 to an undefined 'loop ratio' with no pre-registration; the value is hardcoded in the same commit as any 'live' check, failing even the dome's own weaker definition of prospective prediction. Tag unchanged from c1-c4. (Note: the 'trivial-transcription' subtype of post-hoc that detail_verdict invokes is misapplied — see holes_found #1 — but the underlying post-hoc tag is correct on the more general 'no pre-registration, value sits alongside the 'check' code' criterion.)
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation path from dome geometric parameters (disc radius 20015 km, firmament height 9086 km, sun altitude 5733 km) to 2.66 exists anywhere in the dome repository. The 8/3 = 2.667 structure is consistent with a small-integer gear-ratio parameter from the Spirograph mechanism, not a derivation from dome geometry. Tag unchanged from c1-c4.

holes_found.description: SELF-CONTRADICTION between detail_evidence and detail_verdict. The evidence paragraph explicitly argues 'None of the standard analemma ratios equals 2.66' and lists the five values (height 2.25, width 3.01, area 6.69, perimeter 2.56, A/P 2.61) — none of which is 2.66. The verdict's second paragraph then invokes the trivial-transcription test (Part 3.1b) and asserts 'The numerical value is textbook physics: it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources.' These two statements are mutually inconsistent: the value 2.66 does NOT appear in any undergraduate analemma reference — that is precisely the point of the evidence text. The trivial-transcription test requires the predicted value to 'appear in an undergraduate physics or astronomy textbook (or widely indexed resource such as NIST CODATA, NASA fact sheets, IGRF, USGS datasets, or Wikipedia infoboxes)' (sections.json:part3.html anchor 'posthoc-standard'). 2.66 as an analemma loop ratio appears in none of these. A dome defender can quote our own evidence text back at us to neutralize the post-hoc framing: 'Your own paragraph above says 2.66 doesn't match anything in the textbooks. Now your verdict invokes a test that requires my value to BE in the textbooks. Pick one.' The boilerplate trivial-transcription framing was applied here but does not fit this WIN — the dome's value is fabricated (gear-ratio derived), not textbook-derived. This is a clean rhetorical opening a defender can exploit; it also undermines the consistency of our post-hoc framework across WINs (the test should fail on WIN-019, not pass).
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the second paragraph of detail_verdict_text. Option A (cleanest): drop the trivial-transcription invocation entirely and replace with a tighter post-hoc note grounded in the structural facts the evidence already established — e.g. 'On structural grounds the claim also fails our post-hoc test: the predicted value 2.66 is hardcoded in inject_ai_layer.py with data_source="Spirograph", placed in the same commit as the (non-existent for this WIN) live-check code, with no pre-registration in any timestamping venue independent of the author. The hardcoded value sits where an observation should — that is post-hoc retrodiction by structure, regardless of whether the value also happens to match a sky measurement (it does not; see above).' Option B: keep the trivial-transcription framing but explicitly distinguish 'the number 8/3 is trivial mathematics (true)' from 'the analemma loop ratio is undergraduate-textbook material (false)' — i.e. the dome is doing trivial-transcription of a GEAR-RATIO parameter, not of an analemma property. Either option keeps the post-hoc structural critique while removing the false 'undergraduate references' claim about 2.66.

holes_found.description: TERMINOLOGICAL — 'an 8/3 gear ratio in a hypocycloidal Spirograph produces an 8-cusped epicycloid.' Strictly: a Spirograph (small gear inside ring) is a HYPOCYCLOID mechanism; with R/r = 8/3 in lowest terms (gcd(8,3)=1) the resulting hypocycloid has 8 cusps. Calling that curve an 'epicycloid' is the wrong curve family — epicycloids come from a gear rolling OUTSIDE the ring. The source data file (monitor/analyst/globe-fingerprints/WIN-019.json:spirograph_analysis) has the same conflation ('8-cusped epicycloid (hypocycloid with R/r = 8/3)') so the terminological slip was inherited. The substantive argument (the named mechanism produces a curve with 8 cusps, not 2 lobes) is unchanged. But in a review that explicitly attacks the dome's mathematical literacy, getting epicycloid vs hypocycloid wrong is exactly the kind of small slip a defender can wave around to question our rigor.
holes_found.recommendation: Either say '8-cusped hypocycloid' (consistent with the Spirograph mechanism) or drop the curve-family name and just say '8-cusped closed curve' / 'curve with 8 cusps'. Update globe-fingerprints/WIN-019.json:spirograph_analysis.resulting_curve in the same pass to keep the source data and the rendered text aligned.

holes_found.description: MIXED TOLERANCE ARITHMETIC — the precision paragraph correctly states the implied interval is 'roughly 2.655–2.665 (≈±0.2%)' and that 'any candidate metric must agree to within ±0.2% (the half-width of the implied interval) to count as a match'. Two sentences later the comparison is 'misses by an order of magnitude in fractional terms (3.8% vs. 0.4%)' — 0.4% is the FULL width of the implied interval, not the half-width tolerance just stated. Either comparison is defensible (3.8/0.2 = 19× over half-width, 3.8/0.4 = 9.5× over full-width — both are 'an order of magnitude'), but switching reference within one paragraph invites a defender to ask 'which tolerance is it?'. Minor because the order-of-magnitude conclusion holds under either choice.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one tolerance and stick with it for both the 'must agree' threshold and the 'misses by' comparison. Cleanest single edit: '... must agree to within ±0.2%. The closest primitive metric (perimeter) misses by ~3.8%, an order of magnitude over tolerance.' Drop the explicit '0.4%' figure entirely.

holes_found.description: TLDR CHERRY-PICK (continued from c4) — tldr_evidence lists 'height ~2.25, width ~3.01, perimeter ~2.56' but omits the closest-to-2.66 standard candidate, area-to-perimeter ~2.61 (which is only 1.9% off and would, under a 5%-slop reading, count as a 'match'). The c4 minor hole flagged the TLDR for omitting perimeter; the rewrite added perimeter but not A/P. The TLDR is not actively misleading — perimeter is now included and is the closer primitive metric — but it is still the strongest-possible framing rather than the most honest. A defender reading only the TLDR could accuse us of selection bias.
holes_found.recommendation: Append ', or area-to-perimeter ~2.61' to the tldr_evidence ratio list. One additional clause, ~30 additional characters, no length explosion.

stronger_arguments.description: Authoritative numerical citations for the five standard analemma ratios. The bibliography currently cites Meeus (1998), Holbrow (2013), Lynch (2012), and the NOAA SPA in aggregate, but no specific number is tied to a specific source. Even one direct attribution — e.g. 'Lynch (2012, §3) gives the southern/northern lobe perimeter ratio as 2.56' — would harden the section against 'your numbers came from where exactly?' challenges. Independent recomputation in this c5 review reproduces the five ratios to within 1-3% using the standard EoT formula and a 0.1-day-resolution polyline split at the self-intersection (see quantitative_verification), so the numbers themselves are robust; the issue is rhetorical citation hardening.

Permalink the github citation. The current 'github.com/john09289/predictions/blob/main/inject_ai_layer.py' resolves to whatever the file is today — the dome author can rewrite history (and per CLAUDE.md author-contact status, is aware of our review and may modify the repo reactively). A permalink to a tagged commit SHA (e.g. the V51.1 release commit) would make the citation tamper-resistant, ideally paired with a Wayback Machine snapshot of both the source file and the dome's predictions page at the time we reviewed it.
sources: web.archive.org snapshot of inject_ai_layer.py and predictions page

stronger_arguments.description: Frame the deeper categorical error explicitly. The dome is using a CHARACTER GEOMETRY GENERATOR (Spirograph) as if it were a physics simulation. A Spirograph's parameters are tooth counts on plastic gears chosen by the toymaker for aesthetic curves; a physical analemma's parameters are obliquity (23.44°, a deep dynamical fact about Earth's axial tilt) and orbital eccentricity (0.0167, from Kepler/Newton). Conflating the two is a category error worth naming. Currently we observe the numerical mismatch but do not name what makes the dome's framework wrong category-wise. One sentence on this would help readers who get the analemma math but miss why 'we picked our gear ratio to match' is a fundamentally different kind of claim than 'we derived this from first principles'.

kernel_of_truth.description: The analemma asymmetry IS a real, measurable phenomenon, and the perimeter (2.56) and area-to-perimeter (2.61) ratios DO come close to 2.66 under a generous 5%-slop reading — perimeter at 3.8% off, A/P at 1.9% off, both inside that wider band (see the five-ratio table in data/wins.json:019 detail_evidence). So the dome's value is in the right NEIGHBORHOOD for someone who wants to fit something measurable. The choice of '8/3' as the Spirograph gear ratio is not arbitrary either — picking a small-numerator/small-denominator rational with the right order of magnitude (between 2 and 3) is exactly what one would do to crudely approximate the analemma's overall north-south asymmetry by eye. There is real pattern-matching insight in the choice of mechanism and parameter range, not pure noise.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three reasons the kernel does not save WIN-019: (1) the dome quotes 2.66 to three significant figures, which sets an implied tolerance of ±0.2% (the interval [2.655, 2.665]) — none of the five standard ratios from data/wins.json:019:detail_evidence clears that tolerance, the closest primitive metric being perimeter at 3.8% off; (2) the value matches the gear ratio 8/3 ≈ 2.667 to within 0.25%, roughly 15× better than any sky measurement, and the dome's own inject_ai_layer.py labels the data_source as 'Spirograph' (per direct-inspection citations in WIN-019.c1-c4 code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence), so the natural reading is gear-ratio output rather than measurement; (3) the named mechanism — an 8/3 hypocycloid — produces a curve with 8 cusps, not a 2-lobed figure-8 (recorded in monitor/analyst/globe-fingerprints/WIN-019.json:spirograph_analysis, see hole #2 above for the epicycloid/hypocycloid terminology note), so the dome's 'prediction' is mechanism-falsified at the curve-shape level independent of any tolerance argument.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender who accepts our methodological principles could argue: '2.66 is a property of the GENERATING MECHANISM, not the displayed curve. The dome model claims the analemma is produced by a Spirograph-like mechanism with 8:3 gear teeth, and that gear ratio IS the prediction — the testable claim is that the underlying mechanism has these specific teeth counts. You are testing it against the wrong thing: 2D image properties (area, perimeter, A/P) are downstream of the gear ratio; the gear ratio itself is what we predicted. By analogy, if a physicist predicted the orbital period of a planet from gravitational law, you would not refute him by checking the planet's apparent angular size against his predicted period number. The dome's 8/3 IS testable: does any plausible mechanical model of the analemma have an 8:3 frequency ratio between its two main harmonics?'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Current detail_evidence already addresses this defense in the 'Code-level corroboration' paragraph by noting an 8/3 hypocycloid produces an 8-cusped curve, not a 2-lobed figure-8 — i.e. the named mechanism is mechanism-falsified at the curve-shape level. The defense is essentially refuted there. Optional sharpening: foreground the mechanism-falsification one sentence earlier so it lands before a skimming reader reaches the github-citation appendix material; and pre-empt the 'frequency ratio' framing by noting that the actual analemma decomposes into an annual obliquity term and a (mostly) semi-annual eccentricity term, giving a 2:1 frequency ratio between dominant harmonics — not 8:3. The frequency-ratio rebuttal is the natural next move after we point out the gear count is wrong, and getting ahead of it would tighten the section.

cross_win_consistency.notes: WIN-018 ('analemma day length 6.9 min', verdict 'Misleading') and WIN-020 ('lunar 18.6-yr cycle via gears', verdict 'Std Model Explains') share the analemma / gear-mechanism cluster with WIN-019. WIN-018 makes the same kind of partial-component objection (dome's 6.9 min is the obliquity-only RMS, not the full 8.8 min EoT RMS) that WIN-019 makes about the loop ratio — both treat the analemma as a well-defined phenomenon with known sub-components, both call out the dome for misidentifying which sub-component matches. WIN-020 attacks the 'epicyclic gears' framing on the grounds that the cycle period is an input not a prediction, consistent with WIN-019's attack on '8/3 is a gear-ratio knob not a derivation'. No cross-WIN contradictions found.

claims_checked: implied tolerance interval ±0.2% from three-sig-fig quoting
quantitative_verification.computations: Recomputed the analemma curve from standard formulas: declination(d) = 23.44° · sin(2π·(d-80.25)/365.25), EoT(d) = 9.87·sin(2B) − 7.53·cos(B) − 1.5·sin(B) where B = 2π·(d-81)/365. Sampled at 0.1-day resolution over a full year. Found the figure-8 self-intersection by minimum-distance search between far-separated days: located at (EoT ≈ -0.75 min, dec ≈ +8.92°) — confirming that the figure-8 'waist' is NOT at dec=0 but at dec≈+8.9° (a non-obvious but well-documented fact about the standard analemma). Split the curve at the self-intersection to define the two lobes; used shoelace for area, polyline sum for perimeter, max-min for height/width. Independent results: height ratio 2.23 (review claims 2.25), width ratio 3.12 (claims 3.01), area ratio 6.66 (claims 6.69), perimeter ratio 2.56 (claims 2.56), A/P ratio 2.61 (claims 2.61). All within ~1-3% of the review's claimed values; small differences attribute to the simpler EoT formula and quadrature precision rather than substantive disagreement. The gear-ratio claim 8/3 = 2.6667 and its deviation 0.25% from 2.66 are exact arithmetic.

deeper_analysis: The c4 rewrite was substantial and effective on its targets — it fixed every hole c4 flagged. Quantitative recomputation in this cycle confirms the five-ratio table to within 1-3%, the gear-ratio claim is exact arithmetic, and the spirograph-mechanism falsification (8/3 hypocycloid → 8 cusps not 2 lobes) is geometrically correct. The major finding in c5 is an inherited problem rather than a new bug: the boilerplate 'trivial-transcription test' framing in detail_verdict was copy-pasted from WINs where the dome restates an actual textbook value (e.g. moon distance, Schumann frequency), and it is misapplied here because WIN-019's value 2.66 is precisely NOT a textbook analemma value. The evidence text and the verdict text now talk past each other on this point. The fix is small (rewrite one paragraph) but rhetorically important — it removes a clean opening a dome defender could exploit. The two other minor issues (epicycloid/hypocycloid slip, mixed half/full-width tolerance) are inheritances from the source data and the c4 rewrite respectively, easy to fix in the same pass.
